title: BifurcationAlternative
tex_block_html: Let $G = \lt J, V, E, L \rt$ be a CDMG. For $v, w, c \in V \cup J$: there exists a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ with source $c$ if and only if $v \ne w$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.

% title: BifurcationAlternative
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_5}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_5}

% NOTE on the bifurcation-source convention assumed by this proposition.
%
% Literal LN def 3.4 (Walks, item vi.) writes a bifurcation as
%     $v = v_0 \hut v_1 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = w$
% with $1 \le k \le n$ and source $v_k$ at the directed hinge. The boundary
% case $k = n$ with directed hinge (right arm trivial, single edge
% $w \to v_{n-1}$) would let the source coincide with $w$, contradicting the
% RHS clause $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$ in any acyclic graph
% (and in particular whenever $w \in J$, since by claim \ref{claim_3_1}
% input nodes have no incoming edges, so $\Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) = \{w\}$).
%
% Our def_3_4 encoding has already committed to the strictly-interior-source
% convention via the chapter-init addition recorded in clause (e) of
% def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi. ("each end-node has exactly one arrowhead
% pointing toward it"), which excludes the $k = n$ directed-hinge case. As
% stated in the closing paragraph "Source of the bifurcation" of
% def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., whenever a source $v_k$ is defined we
% automatically have $1 \le k \le n - 1$, so the source is an interior
% vertex distinct from both end-nodes $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$. Under this
% convention the LN proposition below is provable as-stated, and no
% addition_to_the_LN clause is needed for claim_3_5.

\begin{Prp}[BifurcationAlternative]\label{prp:bifurcations_alternative}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we
    use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, the walk definitions of def
    \ref{def:walks}, the family-relationship notation of def
    \ref{def_3_5}, and the hard-intervention construction of def
    \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}. Let
    \[ v,\ w,\ c \;\in\; J \cup V \]
    be three (not necessarily distinct) nodes. All four quantifiers
    ``for every CDMG $G$'', ``for every $v \in J \cup V$'', ``for every
    $w \in J \cup V$'', and ``for every $c \in J \cup V$'' are read
    \emph{universally}, and the biconditional below is asserted for every
    such tuple $(G, v, w, c)$.

    \emph{Hard interventions on singletons.}
    The expressions $G_{\doit(w)}$ and $G_{\doit(v)}$ appearing on the
    right-hand side are read as the singleton-set hard interventions
    $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ and $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$ in the sense of def
    \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} (the LN's lowercase $w$, $v$ inside the
    $\doit(\cdot)$ slot is shorthand for the singleton sets $\{w\}$ and
    $\{v\}$). Since $\{w\}, \{v\} \subseteq J \cup V$ the precondition of
    def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} is met, so both intervened CDMGs
    are defined; and by items i.\ and ii.\ of def
    \ref{def:G_hard_intervention},
    \[
        J_{\doit(\{w\})} \cup V_{\doit(\{w\})}
        \;=\; (J \cup \{w\}) \cup (V \sm \{w\}) \;=\; J \cup V,
    \]
    and analogously for $\{v\}$ in place of $\{w\}$. Consequently
    $\Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(\cdot)$ and $\Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(\cdot)$ (def
    \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv.) are evaluated on the same carrier $J \cup V$
    on which $v$, $w$, $c$ range, and the set-minus clauses
    $\sm \{v\}$ and $\sm \{w\}$ are well-typed.

    \emph{The biconditional.} Propositions (a) and (b) below are
    equivalent.
    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Existence of a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ with
              source $c$.}
              There exists a walk
              \[
                  p \;=\; (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
              \]
              in $G$ from $v$ to $w$ (so $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$, in the
              sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., with some integer
              $n \ge 0$), together with an index $k$ with
              $1 \le k \le n$, such that both of the following hold:
              \begin{itemize}
                  \item $p$ is a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$
                        with witness index $k$, in the sense of def
                        \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., i.e.\ all five
                        clauses (a)--(e) of def \ref{def:walks},
                        item~vi.\ hold for $p$ at the index $k$;
                  \item $c$ is a source of this bifurcation, in the sense
                        of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., closing
                        paragraph ``Source of the bifurcation'', i.e.\
                        clause~(d) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi.\ is
                        realised at $k$ by the \emph{directed}
                        alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$,
                        and $c = v_k$.
              \end{itemize}
              By clause~(e) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., the
              combination of these two requirements forces the witness
              index to satisfy $1 \le k \le n - 1$ (and hence $n \ge 2$);
              so the source $c = v_k$ is automatically an interior vertex
              of $p$, with $c \ne v$ and $c \ne w$.

        \item \emph{Set-theoretic ancestral characterisation.}
              All three of the following clauses hold:
              \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
                  \item $v \ne w$;
                  \item $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$;
                        equivalently, unfolding $\Anc$ via def
                        \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv., and the singleton-set
                        reading of $\doit(w)$ above: \emph{$c$ is an
                        ancestor of $v$ in the do-on-$\{w\}$ intervened
                        CDMG $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$}, i.e.\ there exists a
                        directed walk from $c$ to $v$ in
                        $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ (def \ref{def:walks},
                        item~ii.; def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}),
                        \emph{and} $c \ne v$;
                  \item $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$;
                        equivalently, unfolding $\Anc$ via def
                        \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv., and the singleton-set
                        reading of $\doit(v)$ above: \emph{$c$ is an
                        ancestor of $w$ in the do-on-$\{v\}$ intervened
                        CDMG $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$}, i.e.\ there exists a
                        directed walk from $c$ to $w$ in
                        $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$ (def \ref{def:walks},
                        item~ii.; def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}),
                        \emph{and} $c \ne w$.
              \end{enumerate}
              In LN macro form, (b) is the conjunction
              \[
                  v \ne w \;\land\;
                  c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\} \;\land\;
                  c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\},
              \]
              matching the LN's RHS verbatim.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Prp}

\end{document}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% Drafted starting from the LN proof at lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:366--371
% (four lines, sketching both directions). Expanded to make every step rigorous:
% in (=>) we spell out the decomposition of the bifurcation walk into its two
% directed arms and the lift of each arm to a directed walk in the appropriate
% intervened CDMG; in (<=) we use shortest-walk truncation to obtain end-node
% uniqueness and verify all five clauses (a)--(e) of def \ref{def:walks},
% item~vi., on the concatenated walk.

The proof is by direct construction in both directions, following the LN
proof at \texttt{graphs.tex:366--371} expanded to make every step
rigorous. We use the bifurcation definition (def \ref{def:walks},
item~vi.), the ancestor definition (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv.), and the
hard-intervention construction (def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}). As
discussed in the statement above, $G_{\doit(w)}$ and $G_{\doit(v)}$ are
read as the singleton-set hard interventions $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ and
$G_{\doit(\{v\})}$.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{$(\Rightarrow)$.}\hspace{0.3em}
Assume (a). By definition of a bifurcation with source $c$ (def
\ref{def:walks}, item~vi., and its closing paragraph ``Source of the
bifurcation''), there is a walk
\[
    p \;=\; (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
\]
in $G$ with $v_0 = v$, $v_n = w$, and some integer $n \ge 0$, together
with an index $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$, such that the five clauses
(a)--(e) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., hold at $k$, and clause~(d)
is realised at position $k - 1$ by the \emph{directed} alternative
$a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, with $c = v_k$.

By clause~(e) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi.\ (each end-node has
exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it), combined with the directed
realisation of clause~(d), the witness index is strictly interior:
$1 \le k \le n - 1$. Hence $n \ge 2$, $c = v_k \ne v_0 = v$, and
$c = v_k \ne v_n = w$.

The walk $p$ decomposes at $v_k = c$ into two directed sub-walks in $G$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item the \emph{left arm}
        \[
            L \;:=\; (v_k,\, a_{k-1},\, v_{k-1},\, a_{k-2},\, \dots,\, v_1,\, a_0,\, v_0),
        \]
        which is a directed walk from $c = v_k$ to $v = v_0$ in $G$:
        clause~(b) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., asserts that for
        every $0 \le i \le k - 2$ the edge $a_i$ is the directed edge
        $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (i.e.\ $v_{i+1} \tuh v_i$); the
        hinge $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ from the directed
        alternative of clause~(d) is also directed in this same
        orientation. Read in the order $v_k, v_{k-1}, \dots, v_0$, every
        edge of $L$ is therefore directed forward in that order;
    \item the \emph{right arm}
        \[
            R \;:=\; (v_k,\, a_k,\, v_{k+1},\, a_{k+1},\, \dots,\, v_{n-1},\, a_{n-1},\, v_n),
        \]
        which is a directed walk from $c = v_k$ to $w = v_n$ in $G$:
        clause~(c) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., asserts that for
        every $k \le i \le n - 1$ the edge $a_i$ is the directed edge
        $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$.
\end{itemize}

We must establish the three clauses (i)--(iii) of (b).

\emph{Clause (i): $v \ne w$.}  This is part of clause~(a) of def
\ref{def:walks}, item~vi.

\emph{Clause (ii): $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$.}  We show
that the left arm $L$ remains a directed walk in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$.
By items iii.\ and iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention},
\[
    E_{\doit(\{w\})} \;=\; E \;\sm\; \lC u \tuh w \,\big|\, u \in J \cup V \rC,
\]
i.e.\ $E_{\doit(\{w\})}$ contains every directed edge of $E$ \emph{except}
those whose head is $w$. The set of vertices of $L$ is
$\{v_k, v_{k-1}, \dots, v_0\}$. By clause~(a) of def \ref{def:walks},
item~vi., the bifurcation walk $p$ contains $w = v_n$ \emph{exactly
once}, namely at position $n$. Since $k \le n - 1$, no vertex of $L$
equals $w$. Each edge of $L$ is a directed edge $(v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$
(for some $0 \le i \le k - 1$) whose head $v_i$ is a vertex of $L$,
hence not $w$. Therefore every edge of $L$ lies in $E_{\doit(\{w\})}$,
so $L$ is a directed walk in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ from $c$ to $v$. By def
\ref{def_3_5}, item~iv., this yields $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(\{w\})}}(v)$.
Combined with $c \ne v$ established above,
$c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$.

\emph{Clause (iii): $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.}  The
argument is symmetric. The vertices of $R$ are
$\{v_k, v_{k+1}, \dots, v_n\}$. By clause~(a) of def \ref{def:walks},
item~vi., the walk $p$ contains $v = v_0$ exactly once at position $0$.
Since $k \ge 1$, no vertex of $R$ equals $v$. Each edge of $R$ is a
directed edge $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (for some $k \le i \le n - 1$) whose
head $v_{i+1}$ is a vertex of $R$, hence not $v$. So every edge of $R$
lies in $E_{\doit(\{v\})}$ (by def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}, items
iii.\ and iv.), and $R$ is a directed walk in $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$ from
$c$ to $w$. By def \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv.,
$c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(\{v\})}}(w)$. Combined with $c \ne w$,
$c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.

This completes the $(\Rightarrow)$ direction.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{$(\Leftarrow)$.}\hspace{0.3em}
Assume the three clauses (i)--(iii) of (b). We construct a walk $p$ in
$G$ and an index $k$ at which all five clauses (a)--(e) of def
\ref{def:walks}, item~vi., hold, with clause~(d) realised by the
directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $v_k = c$.

\emph{Step 1: Extract two directed walks via $\Anc$ and pick shortest
representatives.}
By clause (ii) of (b) and def \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv., there exists at
least one directed walk from $c$ to $v$ in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$. Choose
one of \emph{minimum length} and call it
\[
    q_v \;=\; (c = u_0,\; b_0,\; u_1,\; b_1,\; \dots,\; b_{r-1},\; u_r = v),
    \qquad b_j = (u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E_{\doit(\{w\})}.
\]
Since clause (ii) also gives $c \ne v$, we have $r \ge 1$. Symmetrically,
by clause (iii) there exists a directed walk from $c$ to $w$ in
$G_{\doit(\{v\})}$; pick a minimum-length representative
\[
    q_w \;=\; (c = x_0,\; d_0,\; x_1,\; d_1,\; \dots,\; d_{s-1},\; x_s = w),
    \qquad d_j = (x_j, x_{j+1}) \in E_{\doit(\{v\})},
\]
with $s \ge 1$ since $c \ne w$.

\emph{Step 2: $q_v$ and $q_w$ are directed walks in the original $G$.}
By items iii.\ and iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention},
$E_{\doit(\{w\})} \subseteq E$ and $E_{\doit(\{v\})} \subseteq E$ (hard
intervention only removes edges from $E$). Therefore every edge $b_j$ of
$q_v$ and every edge $d_j$ of $q_w$ also lies in $E$, so $q_v$ and $q_w$
are directed walks in $G$ as well (def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.).

\emph{Step 3: Vertex-avoidance properties of $q_v$ and $q_w$.}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(3.\arabic*)]
    \item \emph{$w$ does not appear among the vertices of $q_v$.}  By
        def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}, item~iii., no edge of
        $E_{\doit(\{w\})}$ has head $w$. If $u_j = w$ for some
        $1 \le j \le r$, then the edge
        $b_{j-1} = (u_{j-1}, u_j) \in E_{\doit(\{w\})}$ would be a
        directed edge with head $w$ in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$, contradicting
        item~iii. The remaining possibility $u_0 = w$ is ruled out by
        $u_0 = c \ne w$ (clause (iii) of (b)). Hence
        $w \notin \{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_r\}$.

    \item \emph{$v$ does not appear among $\{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_{r-1}\}$.}
        Suppose, for contradiction, that $u_j = v$ for some
        $0 \le j \le r - 1$. By def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., the
        prefix $(u_0, b_0, u_1, \dots, b_{j-1}, u_j)$ of $q_v$ is itself
        a directed walk (each edge $b_{j'}$ for $0 \le j' \le j - 1$
        retains the form $b_{j'} = (u_{j'}, u_{j'+1}) \in E_{\doit(\{w\})}$).
        It is therefore a directed walk from $c = u_0$ to $v = u_j$ in
        $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ of length $j$. Since $j < r$, this
        contradicts the minimality of $r$ chosen in Step~1. Hence $v$
        only occurs in $q_v$ at position $r$.

    \item \emph{Symmetrically, $v \notin \{x_0, x_1, \dots, x_s\}$ and
        $w \notin \{x_0, x_1, \dots, x_{s-1}\}$.}  The first follows by
        the same head-of-edge argument as in (3.1) with $\{v\}$ in place
        of $\{w\}$ and $q_w$ in place of $q_v$, using clause (ii) of
        (b) for $c \ne v$. The second follows by the same minimality
        argument as in (3.2) with $q_w$, using clause (iii) for
        $c \ne w$.
\end{enumerate}

\emph{Step 4: Construct the candidate walk $p$ by reversing $q_v$ and
concatenating with $q_w$ at the common endpoint $c$.}
Set
\[
    n \;:=\; r + s, \qquad k \;:=\; r.
\]
Define the vertex sequence $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n$ by
\[
    v_i \;:=\; u_{r - i} \quad (0 \le i \le r),
    \qquad
    v_{r + j} \;:=\; x_j \quad (0 \le j \le s),
\]
which is consistent at the shared index $i = r$ (equivalently $j = 0$),
where both formulas give $u_0 = x_0 = c$. In particular
\[
    v_0 \;=\; u_r \;=\; v, \qquad v_k \;=\; v_r \;=\; u_0 \;=\; c, \qquad v_n \;=\; x_s \;=\; w.
\]
Define the edge sequence $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}$ by
\begin{align*}
    a_i \;&:=\; b_{r - 1 - i} \;=\; (u_{r - 1 - i},\, u_{r - i}) \;=\; (v_{i+1},\, v_i) \;\in\; E
        && (0 \le i \le r - 1), \\
    a_{r + j} \;&:=\; d_j \;=\; (x_j,\, x_{j+1}) \;=\; (v_{r+j},\, v_{r+j+1}) \;\in\; E
        && (0 \le j \le s - 1).
\end{align*}
Each $a_i$ lies in $E$ by Step~2, so each $a_i \in E \cup L$ in
particular. By def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.,
$p := (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ is therefore a walk in
$G$ from $v = v_0$ to $w = v_n$.

\emph{Step 5: Verify the five clauses (a)--(e) of def \ref{def:walks},
item~vi., at the split index $k = r$.}

\noindent\emph{Clause (a)} (\emph{$v \ne w$ and end-node uniqueness}).
\begin{itemize}
    \item $v \ne w$ is clause (i) of (b).
    \item $v = v_0$ does not appear at any later position. For
        $1 \le i \le r$ we have $v_i = u_{r - i} \in \{u_0, \dots, u_{r-1}\}$,
        and by Step~3 (3.2), $v \notin \{u_0, \dots, u_{r-1}\}$. For
        $r + 1 \le i \le r + s$ we have
        $v_i = x_{i - r} \in \{x_1, \dots, x_s\}$, and by Step~3 (3.3),
        $v \notin \{x_0, \dots, x_s\}$.
    \item $w = v_n$ does not appear at any earlier position. For
        $0 \le i \le r$ we have
        $v_i = u_{r - i} \in \{u_0, \dots, u_r\}$, and by Step~3 (3.1),
        $w \notin \{u_0, \dots, u_r\}$. For
        $r + 1 \le i \le r + s - 1$ we have
        $v_i = x_{i - r} \in \{x_1, \dots, x_{s-1}\}$, and by Step~3
        (3.3), $w \notin \{x_0, \dots, x_{s-1}\}$.
\end{itemize}

\noindent\emph{Clause (b)} (\emph{the sub-walk
$v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to
$v_0$}).  For $0 \le i \le k - 2 = r - 2$, by Step~4
\[
    a_i \;=\; b_{r - 1 - i} \;=\; (u_{r - 1 - i},\, u_{r - i}) \;=\; (v_{i+1},\, v_i) \;\in\; E,
\]
i.e.\ each such $a_i$ is the directed edge $v_{i+1} \tuh v_i$. Read in
the order $v_{k-1}, v_{k-2}, \dots, v_0$, these edges form a directed
walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks},
item~ii.

\noindent\emph{Clause (c)} (\emph{the sub-walk
$v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$}).
For $k \le i \le n - 1$ (i.e.\ $i = r + j$ with $0 \le j \le s - 1$), by
Step~4
\[
    a_i \;=\; a_{r + j} \;=\; d_j \;=\; (x_j,\, x_{j+1}) \;=\; (v_i,\, v_{i+1}) \;\in\; E,
\]
i.e.\ each such $a_i$ is the directed edge $v_i \tuh v_{i+1}$, and the
right arm is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$ in the sense of def
\ref{def:walks}, item~ii.

\noindent\emph{Clause (d)} (\emph{the hinge edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$,
realised by the directed alternative}).  At index $i = k - 1 = r - 1$,
by Step~4
\[
    a_{k-1} \;=\; b_0 \;=\; (u_0,\, u_1) \;=\; (v_k,\, v_{k-1}) \;\in\; E,
\]
which is the directed edge $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$, equivalently
$v_{k-1} \hut v_k$. Thus clause~(d) is realised at $k - 1$ by the
directed alternative.

\noindent\emph{Clause (e)} (\emph{each end-node has exactly one
arrowhead pointing toward it -- the chapter-init addition recorded in
clause~(e) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi.}).
\begin{itemize}
    \item $v = v_0$ has on the walk $p$ exactly one incident edge,
        namely $a_0$. By Step~4 (the first defining formula, at
        $i = 0$), $a_0 = b_{r-1} = (u_{r-1}, u_r) = (v_1, v_0) \in E$,
        a directed edge with head $v_0$. So $v_0 = v$ has exactly one
        arrowhead pointing toward it. (For $k = 1$ the edge $a_0$ is
        simultaneously the hinge $a_{k-1}$ handled by clause~(d), with
        the same conclusion.)
    \item $w = v_n$ has on the walk $p$ exactly one incident edge,
        namely $a_{n-1}$. By Step~4 (the second defining formula, at
        $j = s - 1$), $a_{n-1} = d_{s-1} = (x_{s-1}, x_s) =
        (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E$, a directed edge with head $v_n$. So
        $v_n = w$ has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it.
    \item The strict-interior consequence $1 \le k \le n - 1$ holds:
        $k = r \ge 1$ since $r \ge 1$ by Step~1, and
        $k = r \le r + s - 1 = n - 1$ since $s \ge 1$ by Step~1.
\end{itemize}

All five clauses (a)--(e) of def \ref{def:walks}, item~vi., are
verified at $k = r$, so $p$ is a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$
with witness index $k$. Clause~(d) was realised by the directed
alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, so by the closing
paragraph ``Source of the bifurcation'' of def \ref{def:walks}, item
vi., the source of this bifurcation is $v_k = c$. This establishes (a).
\end{proof}

\end{document}

Proposition — BifurcationAlternative

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, the walk definitions of def def:walks, the family-relationship notation of def def_3_5, and the hard-intervention construction of def def:G_hard_intervention. Let \[ v,\ w,\ c \;\in\; J \cup V \] be three (not necessarily distinct) nodes. All four quantifiers “for every CDMG $G$”, “for every $v \in J \cup V$”, “for every $w \in J \cup V$”, and “for every $c \in J \cup V$” are read universally, and the biconditional below is asserted for every such tuple $(G, v, w, c)$.

Hard interventions on singletons. The expressions $G_{\doit(w)}$ and $G_{\doit(v)}$ appearing on the right-hand side are read as the singleton-set hard interventions $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ and $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$ in the sense of def def:G_hard_intervention (the LN's lowercase $w$, $v$ inside the $\doit(\cdot)$ slot is shorthand for the singleton sets $\{w\}$ and $\{v\}$). Since $\{w\}, \{v\} \subseteq J \cup V$ the precondition of def def:G_hard_intervention is met, so both intervened CDMGs are defined; and by items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention, \[ J_{\doit(\{w\})} \cup V_{\doit(\{w\})} \;=\; (J \cup \{w\}) \cup (V \sm \{w\}) \;=\; J \cup V, \] and analogously for $\{v\}$ in place of $\{w\}$. Consequently $\Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(\cdot)$ and $\Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(\cdot)$ (def def_3_5, item iv.) are evaluated on the same carrier $J \cup V$ on which $v$, $w$, $c$ range, and the set-minus clauses $\sm \{v\}$ and $\sm \{w\}$ are well-typed.

The biconditional. Propositions (a) and (b) below are equivalent.

  1. Existence of a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ with source $c$. There exists a walk \[ p \;=\; (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ from $v$ to $w$ (so $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$, in the sense of def def:walks, item i., with some integer $n \ge 0$), together with an index $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$, such that both of the following hold:
    • $p$ is a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ with witness index $k$, in the sense of def def:walks, item vi., i.e.\ all five clauses (a)–(e) of def def:walks, item vi.\ hold for $p$ at the index $k$;
    • $c$ is a source of this bifurcation, in the sense of def def:walks, item vi., closing paragraph “Source of the bifurcation”, i.e.\ clause (d) of def def:walks, item vi.\ is realised at $k$ by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, and $c = v_k$.

    By clause (e) of def def:walks, item vi., the combination of these two requirements forces the witness index to satisfy $1 \le k \le n - 1$ (and hence $n \ge 2$); so the source $c = v_k$ is automatically an interior vertex of $p$, with $c \ne v$ and $c \ne w$.

  2. Set-theoretic ancestral characterisation. All three of the following clauses hold:

    1. $v \ne w$;
    2. $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$; equivalently, unfolding $\Anc$ via def def_3_5, item iv., and the singleton-set reading of $\doit(w)$ above: $c$ is an ancestor of $v$ in the do-on-$\{w\}$ intervened CDMG $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$, i.e.\ there exists a directed walk from $c$ to $v$ in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ (def def:walks, item ii.; def def:G_hard_intervention), and $c \ne v$;
    3. $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$; equivalently, unfolding $\Anc$ via def def_3_5, item iv., and the singleton-set reading of $\doit(v)$ above: $c$ is an ancestor of $w$ in the do-on-$\{v\}$ intervened CDMG $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$, i.e.\ there exists a directed walk from $c$ to $w$ in $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$ (def def:walks, item ii.; def def:G_hard_intervention), and $c \ne w$.

    In LN macro form, (b) is the conjunction \[ v \ne w \;\land\; c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\} \;\land\; c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}, \] matching the LN's RHS verbatim.

Proof.

The proof is by direct construction in both directions, following the LN proof at graphs.tex:366–371 expanded to make every step rigorous. We use the bifurcation definition (def def:walks, item vi.), the ancestor definition (def def_3_5, item iv.), and the hard-intervention construction (def def:G_hard_intervention). As discussed in the statement above, $G_{\doit(w)}$ and $G_{\doit(v)}$ are read as the singleton-set hard interventions $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ and $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$.

$(\Rightarrow)$.\hspace{0.3em} Assume (a). By definition of a bifurcation with source $c$ (def def:walks, item vi., and its closing paragraph “Source of the bifurcation”), there is a walk \[ p \;=\; (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ with $v_0 = v$, $v_n = w$, and some integer $n \ge 0$, together with an index $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$, such that the five clauses (a)–(e) of def def:walks, item vi., hold at $k$, and clause (d) is realised at position $k - 1$ by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, with $c = v_k$.

By clause (e) of def def:walks, item vi.\ (each end-node has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it), combined with the directed realisation of clause (d), the witness index is strictly interior: $1 \le k \le n - 1$. Hence $n \ge 2$, $c = v_k \ne v_0 = v$, and $c = v_k \ne v_n = w$.

The walk $p$ decomposes at $v_k = c$ into two directed sub-walks in $G$:

  • the left arm \[ L \;:=\; (v_k,\, a_{k-1},\, v_{k-1},\, a_{k-2},\, \dots,\, v_1,\, a_0,\, v_0), \] which is a directed walk from $c = v_k$ to $v = v_0$ in $G$: clause (b) of def def:walks, item vi., asserts that for every $0 \le i \le k - 2$ the edge $a_i$ is the directed edge $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (i.e.\ $v_{i+1} \tuh v_i$); the hinge $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ from the directed alternative of clause (d) is also directed in this same orientation. Read in the order $v_k, v_{k-1}, \dots, v_0$, every edge of $L$ is therefore directed forward in that order;
  • the right arm \[ R \;:=\; (v_k,\, a_k,\, v_{k+1},\, a_{k+1},\, \dots,\, v_{n-1},\, a_{n-1},\, v_n), \] which is a directed walk from $c = v_k$ to $w = v_n$ in $G$: clause (c) of def def:walks, item vi., asserts that for every $k \le i \le n - 1$ the edge $a_i$ is the directed edge $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$.

We must establish the three clauses (i)–(iii) of (b).

Clause (i): $v \ne w$. This is part of clause (a) of def def:walks, item vi.

Clause (ii): $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$. We show that the left arm $L$ remains a directed walk in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$. By items iii.\ and iv.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention, \[ E_{\doit(\{w\})} \;=\; E \;\sm\; \lC u \tuh w \,\big|\, u \in J \cup V \rC, \] i.e.\ $E_{\doit(\{w\})}$ contains every directed edge of $E$ except those whose head is $w$. The set of vertices of $L$ is $\{v_k, v_{k-1}, \dots, v_0\}$. By clause (a) of def def:walks, item vi., the bifurcation walk $p$ contains $w = v_n$ exactly once, namely at position $n$. Since $k \le n - 1$, no vertex of $L$ equals $w$. Each edge of $L$ is a directed edge $(v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (for some $0 \le i \le k - 1$) whose head $v_i$ is a vertex of $L$, hence not $w$. Therefore every edge of $L$ lies in $E_{\doit(\{w\})}$, so $L$ is a directed walk in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ from $c$ to $v$. By def def_3_5, item iv., this yields $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(\{w\})}}(v)$. Combined with $c \ne v$ established above, $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$.

Clause (iii): $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$. The argument is symmetric. The vertices of $R$ are $\{v_k, v_{k+1}, \dots, v_n\}$. By clause (a) of def def:walks, item vi., the walk $p$ contains $v = v_0$ exactly once at position $0$. Since $k \ge 1$, no vertex of $R$ equals $v$. Each edge of $R$ is a directed edge $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (for some $k \le i \le n - 1$) whose head $v_{i+1}$ is a vertex of $R$, hence not $v$. So every edge of $R$ lies in $E_{\doit(\{v\})}$ (by def def:G_hard_intervention, items iii.\ and iv.), and $R$ is a directed walk in $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$ from $c$ to $w$. By def def_3_5, item iv., $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(\{v\})}}(w)$. Combined with $c \ne w$, $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.

This completes the $(\Rightarrow)$ direction.

$(\Leftarrow)$.\hspace{0.3em} Assume the three clauses (i)–(iii) of (b). We construct a walk $p$ in $G$ and an index $k$ at which all five clauses (a)–(e) of def def:walks, item vi., hold, with clause (d) realised by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $v_k = c$.

Step 1: Extract two directed walks via $\Anc$ and pick shortest representatives. By clause (ii) of (b) and def def_3_5, item iv., there exists at least one directed walk from $c$ to $v$ in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$. Choose one of minimum length and call it \[ q_v \;=\; (c = u_0,\; b_0,\; u_1,\; b_1,\; \dots,\; b_{r-1},\; u_r = v), \qquad b_j = (u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E_{\doit(\{w\})}. \] Since clause (ii) also gives $c \ne v$, we have $r \ge 1$. Symmetrically, by clause (iii) there exists a directed walk from $c$ to $w$ in $G_{\doit(\{v\})}$; pick a minimum-length representative \[ q_w \;=\; (c = x_0,\; d_0,\; x_1,\; d_1,\; \dots,\; d_{s-1},\; x_s = w), \qquad d_j = (x_j, x_{j+1}) \in E_{\doit(\{v\})}, \] with $s \ge 1$ since $c \ne w$.

Step 2: $q_v$ and $q_w$ are directed walks in the original $G$. By items iii.\ and iv.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention, $E_{\doit(\{w\})} \subseteq E$ and $E_{\doit(\{v\})} \subseteq E$ (hard intervention only removes edges from $E$). Therefore every edge $b_j$ of $q_v$ and every edge $d_j$ of $q_w$ also lies in $E$, so $q_v$ and $q_w$ are directed walks in $G$ as well (def def:walks, item ii.).

Step 3: Vertex-avoidance properties of $q_v$ and $q_w$.

  1. $w$ does not appear among the vertices of $q_v$. By def def:G_hard_intervention, item iii., no edge of $E_{\doit(\{w\})}$ has head $w$. If $u_j = w$ for some $1 \le j \le r$, then the edge $b_{j-1} = (u_{j-1}, u_j) \in E_{\doit(\{w\})}$ would be a directed edge with head $w$ in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$, contradicting item iii. The remaining possibility $u_0 = w$ is ruled out by $u_0 = c \ne w$ (clause (iii) of (b)). Hence $w \notin \{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_r\}$.
  2. $v$ does not appear among $\{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_{r-1}\}$. Suppose, for contradiction, that $u_j = v$ for some $0 \le j \le r - 1$. By def def:walks, item ii., the prefix $(u_0, b_0, u_1, \dots, b_{j-1}, u_j)$ of $q_v$ is itself a directed walk (each edge $b_{j'}$ for $0 \le j' \le j - 1$ retains the form $b_{j'} = (u_{j'}, u_{j'+1}) \in E_{\doit(\{w\})}$). It is therefore a directed walk from $c = u_0$ to $v = u_j$ in $G_{\doit(\{w\})}$ of length $j$. Since $j < r$, this contradicts the minimality of $r$ chosen in Step 1. Hence $v$ only occurs in $q_v$ at position $r$.
  3. Symmetrically, $v \notin \{x_0, x_1, \dots, x_s\}$ and $w \notin \{x_0, x_1, \dots, x_{s-1}\}$. The first follows by the same head-of-edge argument as in (3.1) with $\{v\}$ in place of $\{w\}$ and $q_w$ in place of $q_v$, using clause (ii) of (b) for $c \ne v$. The second follows by the same minimality argument as in (3.2) with $q_w$, using clause (iii) for $c \ne w$.

Step 4: Construct the candidate walk $p$ by reversing $q_v$ and concatenating with $q_w$ at the common endpoint $c$. Set \[ n \;:=\; r + s, \qquad k \;:=\; r. \] Define the vertex sequence $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n$ by \[ v_i \;:=\; u_{r - i} \quad (0 \le i \le r), \qquad v_{r + j} \;:=\; x_j \quad (0 \le j \le s), \] which is consistent at the shared index $i = r$ (equivalently $j = 0$), where both formulas give $u_0 = x_0 = c$. In particular \[ v_0 \;=\; u_r \;=\; v, \qquad v_k \;=\; v_r \;=\; u_0 \;=\; c, \qquad v_n \;=\; x_s \;=\; w. \] Define the edge sequence $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}$ by \[\begin{aligned} a_i \;&:=\; b_{r - 1 - i} \;=\; (u_{r - 1 - i},\, u_{r - i}) \;=\; (v_{i+1},\, v_i) \;\in\; E && (0 \le i \le r - 1), \\ a_{r + j} \;&:=\; d_j \;=\; (x_j,\, x_{j+1}) \;=\; (v_{r+j},\, v_{r+j+1}) \;\in\; E && (0 \le j \le s - 1). \end{aligned}\] Each $a_i$ lies in $E$ by Step 2, so each $a_i \in E \cup L$ in particular. By def def:walks, item i., $p := (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ is therefore a walk in $G$ from $v = v_0$ to $w = v_n$.

Step 5: Verify the five clauses (a)–(e) of def def:walks, item vi., at the split index $k = r$.

Clause (a) ($v \ne w$ and end-node uniqueness).

  • $v \ne w$ is clause (i) of (b).
  • $v = v_0$ does not appear at any later position. For $1 \le i \le r$ we have $v_i = u_{r - i} \in \{u_0, \dots, u_{r-1}\}$, and by Step 3 (3.2), $v \notin \{u_0, \dots, u_{r-1}\}$. For $r + 1 \le i \le r + s$ we have $v_i = x_{i - r} \in \{x_1, \dots, x_s\}$, and by Step 3 (3.3), $v \notin \{x_0, \dots, x_s\}$.
  • $w = v_n$ does not appear at any earlier position. For $0 \le i \le r$ we have $v_i = u_{r - i} \in \{u_0, \dots, u_r\}$, and by Step 3 (3.1), $w \notin \{u_0, \dots, u_r\}$. For $r + 1 \le i \le r + s - 1$ we have $v_i = x_{i - r} \in \{x_1, \dots, x_{s-1}\}$, and by Step 3 (3.3), $w \notin \{x_0, \dots, x_{s-1}\}$.

Clause (b) (the sub-walk $v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$). For $0 \le i \le k - 2 = r - 2$, by Step 4 \[ a_i \;=\; b_{r - 1 - i} \;=\; (u_{r - 1 - i},\, u_{r - i}) \;=\; (v_{i+1},\, v_i) \;\in\; E, \] i.e.\ each such $a_i$ is the directed edge $v_{i+1} \tuh v_i$. Read in the order $v_{k-1}, v_{k-2}, \dots, v_0$, these edges form a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$ in the sense of def def:walks, item ii.

Clause (c) (the sub-walk $v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$). For $k \le i \le n - 1$ (i.e.\ $i = r + j$ with $0 \le j \le s - 1$), by Step 4 \[ a_i \;=\; a_{r + j} \;=\; d_j \;=\; (x_j,\, x_{j+1}) \;=\; (v_i,\, v_{i+1}) \;\in\; E, \] i.e.\ each such $a_i$ is the directed edge $v_i \tuh v_{i+1}$, and the right arm is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$ in the sense of def def:walks, item ii.

Clause (d) (the hinge edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$, realised by the directed alternative). At index $i = k - 1 = r - 1$, by Step 4 \[ a_{k-1} \;=\; b_0 \;=\; (u_0,\, u_1) \;=\; (v_k,\, v_{k-1}) \;\in\; E, \] which is the directed edge $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$, equivalently $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$. Thus clause (d) is realised at $k - 1$ by the directed alternative.

Clause (e) (each end-node has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it – the chapter-init addition recorded in clause (e) of def def:walks, item vi.).

  • $v = v_0$ has on the walk $p$ exactly one incident edge, namely $a_0$. By Step 4 (the first defining formula, at $i = 0$), $a_0 = b_{r-1} = (u_{r-1}, u_r) = (v_1, v_0) \in E$, a directed edge with head $v_0$. So $v_0 = v$ has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it. (For $k = 1$ the edge $a_0$ is simultaneously the hinge $a_{k-1}$ handled by clause (d), with the same conclusion.)
  • $w = v_n$ has on the walk $p$ exactly one incident edge, namely $a_{n-1}$. By Step 4 (the second defining formula, at $j = s - 1$), $a_{n-1} = d_{s-1} = (x_{s-1}, x_s) = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E$, a directed edge with head $v_n$. So $v_n = w$ has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it.
  • The strict-interior consequence $1 \le k \le n - 1$ holds: $k = r \ge 1$ since $r \ge 1$ by Step 1, and $k = r \le r + s - 1 = n - 1$ since $s \ge 1$ by Step 1.

All five clauses (a)–(e) of def def:walks, item vi., are verified at $k = r$, so $p$ is a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ with witness index $k$. Clause (d) was realised by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, so by the closing paragraph “Source of the bifurcation” of def def:walks, item vi., the source of this bifurcation is $v_k = c$. This establishes (a).

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in this file, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on each helper lemma and on `bifurcationAlternative`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing throughout `claim_3_5`: the proof builds `Walk` values by `Walk.cons` and recurses over `Finset.filter`/`Finset.union`/`Finset.sdiff` applied to $G.J$, $G.V$, and the singleton intervention sets $\{v\}$, $\{w\}$, and the singleton-subset lift `Finset.singleton_subset_iff.mpr` used in the theorem statement all require decidable equality. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g. `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits across this file; decidability drives `Walk` recursion, `Finset.filter`/`union`/`sdiff`, and `Finset.singleton_subset_iff` in the main theorem.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
/-- Forward direction of the carrier-matching equality
`(G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W) = G.J ∪ G.V`: every node of the intervened
CDMG is a node of `G`.  Consumes `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` to fold the
`W`-half of the left disjunct into `G.J ∪ G.V`.  Verbatim copy of
`AcyclicPreservedUnderDo.lean`'s lemma of the same name; localised
here because the sibling copy is `private`. -/
private lemma mem_of_mem_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} {v : Node}
    (h : v ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW) : v ∈ G := by
  -- `v ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW` reduces by the `Membership`
  -- instance from `def_3_2` to `v ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)`.
  change v ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W) at h
  change v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V
  rcases Finset.mem_union.mp h with hJW | hVW
  · rcases Finset.mem_union.mp hJW with hJ | hWmem
    · exact Finset.mem_union_left _ hJ
    · exact hW hWmem
  · exact Finset.mem_union_right _ (Finset.mem_sdiff.mp hVW).1

/-- Per-edge content of the (⇐) direction's walk lift: any walk-step
in `G.hardInterventionOn W hW` is also a walk-step in `G`.  Both
`E_{do(W)} ⊆ E` and `L_{do(W)} ⊆ L` follow from `Finset.filter_subset`,
applied pointwise.  Verbatim copy of `AcyclicPreservedUnderDo.lean`'s
lemma of the same name. -/
private lemma Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V}
    {u v : Node} {a : Node × Node}
    (h : (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).WalkStep u a v) :
    G.WalkStep u a v := by
  rcases h with ⟨ha, hELor⟩ | ⟨ha, hE⟩
  · refine Or.inl ⟨ha, ?_⟩
    rcases hELor with hE | hL
    · exact Or.inl (Finset.mem_filter.mp hE).1
    · exact Or.inr (Finset.mem_filter.mp hL).1
  · exact Or.inr ⟨ha, (Finset.mem_filter.mp hE).1⟩

/-- The walk-lift, as a recursive function on `Walk`s: a walk in the
intervened CDMG `G.hardInterventionOn W hW` is *the same tuple*
viewed as a walk in `G`.  Each `cons` cell keeps its vertex `v` and
its edge `a`; only the `WalkStep` witness is replaced by its lift
through `Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn`. -/
private def Walk.liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v → Walk G u v
  | _, _, .nil w hw =>
      Walk.nil w (mem_of_mem_hardInterventionOn hw)
  | _, _, .cons vMid a h p =>
      Walk.cons vMid a
        (Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn h)
        (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention p)

/-- The walk-lift preserves `IsDirectedWalk`: the per-edge constraint
`a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E` from `def_3_4` item ii survives the lift
because `(G.hardInterventionOn W hW).E ⊆ G.E` by
`Finset.mem_filter`. -/
private lemma Walk.isDirectedWalk_liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      p.IsDirectedWalk →
        (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention (hW := hW) p).IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => trivial
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, hp => by
      obtain ⟨ha_eq, ha_E, hp_dir⟩ := hp
      refine ⟨ha_eq, (Finset.mem_filter.mp ha_E).1, ?_⟩
      exact isDirectedWalk_liftFromHardIntervention p hp_dir

/-- The walk-lift preserves `length`: each `cons` cell of the input
walk produces exactly one `cons` cell of the output walk, with the
same vertex / edge data. -/
private lemma Walk.length_liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention (hW := hW) p).length = p.length
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => rfl
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ p =>
      congrArg (· + 1) (length_liftFromHardIntervention p)

/-- **New (subtask 1 of `claim_3_5`):** the walk-lift preserves the
underlying `vertices` list.  Each `cons` cell of the input keeps its
vertex data verbatim under the lift, so the induced list of vertices
is byte-identical.  The `nil` case reduces to `[v] = [v]` definitionally;
the `cons` case is `u :: (lift p).vertices = u :: p.vertices` via
`congrArg (u :: ·) ih`.  Used by the (⇐) direction's clause~(a) /
clause~(e) end-node-uniqueness bookkeeping when lifting the minimum-
length directed arms `q_v`, `q_w` back to `G`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention (hW := hW) p).vertices = p.vertices
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => rfl
  | u, _, .cons _ _ _ p =>
      congrArg (u :: ·) (vertices_liftFromHardIntervention p)
lean_blocks.explanation: This block re-localises the `(⇒)`-direction walk-lift toolkit from `AcyclicPreservedUnderDo.lean`, whose copies are `private`. `mem_of_mem_hardInterventionOn` is the forward half of the carrier equality $(G.J \cup W) \cup (G.V \setminus W) = G.J \cup G.V$, consuming `hW : W \subseteq G.J \cup G.V` to fold the `W`-summand back into `G.J \cup G.V`. `Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn` lifts a single edge: both `E_{do(W)} \subseteq E` and `L_{do(W)} \subseteq L` are read off `Finset.filter_subset` via `Finset.mem_filter.mp`. The recursive function `Walk.liftFromHardIntervention` traverses the `Walk` constructor by constructor — same vertices, same edges, only the `WalkStep` witness is replaced — and the three companion lemmas (`isDirectedWalk_lift…`, `length_lift…`, `vertices_lift…`) certify that `IsDirectedWalk`, edge count, and vertex list survive verbatim. The vertex-preservation lemma is **subtask 1**: downstream (claim_3_5's `(⇐)` direction) the lifted minimum-length arms $q_v$, $q_w$ keep their end-node bookkeeping intact, so the LN's clause (a) and clause (e) constraints on the assembled bifurcation walk translate without reshuffling.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
/-- Forward direction of the carrier-matching equality
`(G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W) = G.J ∪ G.V`: every node of the intervened
CDMG is a node of `G`.  Consumes `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` to fold the
`W`-half of the left disjunct into `G.J ∪ G.V`.  Verbatim copy of
`AcyclicPreservedUnderDo.lean`'s lemma of the same name; localised
here because the sibling copy is `private`. -/
private lemma mem_of_mem_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} {v : Node}
    (h : v ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW) : v ∈ G := by
  -- `v ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW` reduces by the `Membership`
  -- instance from `def_3_2` to `v ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)`.
  change v ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W) at h
  -- Target: rewrite `v ∈ G` to the underlying `G.J ∪ G.V`.
  change v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V
  -- Split the outer union into the `G.J ∪ W` and `G.V \ W` summands.
  rcases Finset.mem_union.mp h with hJW | hVW
  -- Left summand: further split into `G.J` vs `W`.
  · rcases Finset.mem_union.mp hJW with hJ | hWmem
    -- `v ∈ G.J` lands in the left of the target union directly.
    · exact Finset.mem_union_left _ hJ
    -- `v ∈ W` folds back via `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`.
    · exact hW hWmem
  -- `v ∈ G.V \ W`: the first conjunct of `mem_sdiff` is `v ∈ G.V`.
  · exact Finset.mem_union_right _ (Finset.mem_sdiff.mp hVW).1

/-- Per-edge content of the (⇐) direction's walk lift: any walk-step
in `G.hardInterventionOn W hW` is also a walk-step in `G`.  Both
`E_{do(W)} ⊆ E` and `L_{do(W)} ⊆ L` follow from `Finset.filter_subset`,
applied pointwise.  Verbatim copy of `AcyclicPreservedUnderDo.lean`'s
lemma of the same name. -/
private lemma Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V}
    {u v : Node} {a : Node × Node}
    (h : (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).WalkStep u a v) :
    G.WalkStep u a v := by
  -- `WalkStep` has two disjuncts: forward (E or L) vs backward (E).
  rcases h with ⟨ha, hELor⟩ | ⟨ha, hE⟩
  -- Forward branch: edge equation `ha` survives; rebuild the inner Or.
  · refine Or.inl ⟨ha, ?_⟩
    rcases hELor with hE | hL
    -- Filtered `E`-membership projects back to plain `G.E` membership.
    · exact Or.inl (Finset.mem_filter.mp hE).1
    -- Same for the bidirected `L` set.
    · exact Or.inr (Finset.mem_filter.mp hL).1
  -- Backward branch: identical filter-projection on `E`.
  · exact Or.inr ⟨ha, (Finset.mem_filter.mp hE).1⟩

/-- The walk-lift, as a recursive function on `Walk`s: a walk in the
intervened CDMG `G.hardInterventionOn W hW` is *the same tuple*
viewed as a walk in `G`.  Each `cons` cell keeps its vertex `v` and
its edge `a`; only the `WalkStep` witness is replaced by its lift
through `Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn`. -/
private def Walk.liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v → Walk G u v
  -- `nil` cell: only the source-membership proof needs lifting.
  | _, _, .nil w hw =>
      Walk.nil w (mem_of_mem_hardInterventionOn hw)
  -- `cons` cell: vertex `vMid` and edge `a` carry over; lift the step and recurse on the tail.
  | _, _, .cons vMid a h p =>
      Walk.cons vMid a
        (Walk.liftWalkStep_of_hardInterventionOn h)
        (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention p)

/-- The walk-lift preserves `IsDirectedWalk`: the per-edge constraint
`a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E` from `def_3_4` item ii survives the lift
because `(G.hardInterventionOn W hW).E ⊆ G.E` by
`Finset.mem_filter`. -/
private lemma Walk.isDirectedWalk_liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      p.IsDirectedWalk →
        (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention (hW := hW) p).IsDirectedWalk
  -- `nil` walk is trivially directed.
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => trivial
  -- `cons` walk: unpack the head edge data and recurse on the tail.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, hp => by
      obtain ⟨ha_eq, ha_E, hp_dir⟩ := hp
      -- Head `E`-membership lifts via the first projection of `mem_filter`.
      refine ⟨ha_eq, (Finset.mem_filter.mp ha_E).1, ?_⟩
      exact isDirectedWalk_liftFromHardIntervention p hp_dir

/-- The walk-lift preserves `length`: each `cons` cell of the input
walk produces exactly one `cons` cell of the output walk, with the
same vertex / edge data. -/
private lemma Walk.length_liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention (hW := hW) p).length = p.length
  -- `nil` walks both have length 0.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => rfl
  -- `cons` walks: `+1` propagates through the IH via `congrArg`.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ p =>
      congrArg (· + 1) (length_liftFromHardIntervention p)

/-- **New (subtask 1 of `claim_3_5`):** the walk-lift preserves the
underlying `vertices` list.  Each `cons` cell of the input keeps its
vertex data verbatim under the lift, so the induced list of vertices
is byte-identical.  The `nil` case reduces to `[v] = [v]` definitionally;
the `cons` case is `u :: (lift p).vertices = u :: p.vertices` via
`congrArg (u :: ·) ih`.  Used by the (⇐) direction's clause~(a) /
clause~(e) end-node-uniqueness bookkeeping when lifting the minimum-
length directed arms `q_v`, `q_w` back to `G`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_liftFromHardIntervention
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      (Walk.liftFromHardIntervention (hW := hW) p).vertices = p.vertices
  -- `nil` cell: `[v] = [v]` by definitional reduction.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => rfl
  -- `cons` cell: head `u` preserved; tail handled by induction via `congrArg (u :: ·) ih`.
  | u, _, .cons _ _ _ p =>
      congrArg (u :: ·) (vertices_liftFromHardIntervention p)

lean_blocks.code:
/-- Concatenate two walks `p : Walk G u v` and `q : Walk G v w` into a
walk `Walk G u w`.  The `nil` case forwards `q` unchanged; the `cons`
case recurses on the tail and re-attaches the head edge.  Verbatim copy
of `AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`'s `private Walk.comp`; re-declared
locally because the sibling copy is `private`. -/
private def Walk.comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node}, Walk G u v → Walk G v w → Walk G u w
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, q => q
  | _, _, _, .cons v a h p, q => .cons v a h (p.comp q)

/-- `Walk.comp` is additive on lengths: the number of edges of the
concatenation equals the sum of the two arms' edge counts.  Verbatim
copy of the same-named `private` lemma in
`AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`. -/
private lemma Walk.length_comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (q : Walk G v w),
      (p.comp q).length = p.length + q.length
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, q => by
      simp [Walk.comp, Walk.length]
  | _, _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, q => by
      simp [Walk.comp, Walk.length, Walk.length_comp p q,
            Nat.add_comm, Nat.add_left_comm]

/-- `Walk.comp` preserves `IsDirectedWalk` when both arms are directed:
the per-edge `def_3_4` item~ii constraint `a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E` is
preserved cell-by-cell along the recursion.  Verbatim copy of the
same-named `private` lemma in `AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`. -/
private lemma Walk.isDirectedWalk_comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (q : Walk G v w),
      p.IsDirectedWalk → q.IsDirectedWalk → (p.comp q).IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, _, _, hq => hq
  | _, _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, q, hp, hq => by
      obtain ⟨h1, h2, h3⟩ := hp
      exact ⟨h1, h2, Walk.isDirectedWalk_comp p q h3 hq⟩

/-- Auxiliary: every walk's `vertices` list is non-empty.  The `nil`
walk gives `[v]`; every `cons` cell prepends a new head vertex.  Needed
by `Walk.vertices_comp`'s `cons` case to discharge the side condition
of `List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_ne_nil {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v), p.vertices ≠ []
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]

/-- **New (subtask 2 of `claim_3_5`):** `Walk.comp` interacts with
`vertices` by dropping the last vertex of the left arm and
concatenating with the full vertex list of the right arm.  This is the
load-bearing bookkeeping lemma for the (⇐) direction: the candidate
bifurcation walk built from `(reverse q_v).comp q_w` has vertex list
`(reverse q_v).vertices.dropLast ++ q_w.vertices`, and end-node /
interior-membership conditions like `v ∉ p.vertices.tail` or
`v ∈ p.vertices` reduce to per-arm membership statements via this
equation.

The `nil` case closes by `rfl`: `[v].dropLast = []` and `[] ++ _ = _`
are both definitionally true.  The `cons` case applies the inductive
hypothesis and uses `List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil
(Walk.vertices_ne_nil p)` to unfold `(u :: p.vertices).dropLast`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (q : Walk G v w),
      (p.comp q).vertices = p.vertices.dropLast ++ q.vertices
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, _ => rfl
  | _, _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, q => by
      have hne : p.vertices ≠ [] := Walk.vertices_ne_nil p
      simp [Walk.comp, Walk.vertices, Walk.vertices_comp p q,
            List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil hne]
lean_blocks.explanation: This block re-declares `Walk.comp` — concatenation of two walks `p : Walk G u v` and `q : Walk G v w` into a `Walk G u w` — together with its three companion lemmas. `comp` itself is a verbatim structural-recursion copy of the same-named `private` function in `AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`: the `nil` arm forwards `q` unchanged, the `cons` arm prepends the head edge and recurses on the tail. `length_comp` and `isDirectedWalk_comp` carry the obvious additive / pointwise preservation. The two new pieces are `Walk.vertices_ne_nil` (every walk's vertex list has at least one entry, the source) and **subtask 2's** load-bearing `Walk.vertices_comp`, which states `(p.comp q).vertices = p.vertices.dropLast ++ q.vertices`. The dropLast is essential because `q`'s vertex list already contains the shared endpoint $v$ at its head, so trimming $v$ off `p`'s tail avoids double-counting. The `cons` case of the proof unfolds `(u :: p.vertices).dropLast` via mathlib's `List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil`, which is precisely why `vertices_ne_nil` is needed as the side-condition discharger. Consumers in subtasks 5 and 7 use `vertices_comp` to reduce membership questions on the assembled bifurcation walk to per-arm questions.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
/-- Concatenate two walks `p : Walk G u v` and `q : Walk G v w` into a
walk `Walk G u w`.  The `nil` case forwards `q` unchanged; the `cons`
case recurses on the tail and re-attaches the head edge.  Verbatim copy
of `AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`'s `private Walk.comp`; re-declared
locally because the sibling copy is `private`. -/
private def Walk.comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node}, Walk G u v → Walk G v w → Walk G u w
  -- `nil` on the left: pass `q` straight through.
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, q => q
  -- `cons` on the left: keep the head edge, recurse on the tail.
  | _, _, _, .cons v a h p, q => .cons v a h (p.comp q)

/-- `Walk.comp` is additive on lengths: the number of edges of the
concatenation equals the sum of the two arms' edge counts.  Verbatim
copy of the same-named `private` lemma in
`AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`. -/
private lemma Walk.length_comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (q : Walk G v w),
      (p.comp q).length = p.length + q.length
  -- `nil` arm: `0 + q.length = q.length` by `simp`.
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, q => by
      simp [Walk.comp, Walk.length]
  -- `cons` arm: peel one edge, recurse, reassociate via `Nat.add_*`.
  | _, _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, q => by
      simp [Walk.comp, Walk.length, Walk.length_comp p q,
            Nat.add_comm, Nat.add_left_comm]

/-- `Walk.comp` preserves `IsDirectedWalk` when both arms are directed:
the per-edge `def_3_4` item~ii constraint `a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E` is
preserved cell-by-cell along the recursion.  Verbatim copy of the
same-named `private` lemma in `AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder.lean`. -/
private lemma Walk.isDirectedWalk_comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (q : Walk G v w),
      p.IsDirectedWalk → q.IsDirectedWalk → (p.comp q).IsDirectedWalk
  -- `nil` arm: the result is just `q`, so `hq` is what we need.
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, _, _, hq => hq
  -- `cons` arm: unpack the head triple of `hp` and recurse on the tail.
  | _, _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, q, hp, hq => by
      obtain ⟨h1, h2, h3⟩ := hp
      exact ⟨h1, h2, Walk.isDirectedWalk_comp p q h3 hq⟩

/-- Auxiliary: every walk's `vertices` list is non-empty.  The `nil`
walk gives `[v]`; every `cons` cell prepends a new head vertex.  Needed
by `Walk.vertices_comp`'s `cons` case to discharge the side condition
of `List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_ne_nil {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v), p.vertices ≠ []
  -- `nil`: `[v] ≠ []`.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]
  -- `cons`: vertex list is `u :: …`, automatically non-empty.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]

/-- **New (subtask 2 of `claim_3_5`):** `Walk.comp` interacts with
`vertices` by dropping the last vertex of the left arm and
concatenating with the full vertex list of the right arm.  This is the
load-bearing bookkeeping lemma for the (⇐) direction: the candidate
bifurcation walk built from `(reverse q_v).comp q_w` has vertex list
`(reverse q_v).vertices.dropLast ++ q_w.vertices`, and end-node /
interior-membership conditions like `v ∉ p.vertices.tail` or
`v ∈ p.vertices` reduce to per-arm membership statements via this
equation.

The `nil` case closes by `rfl`: `[v].dropLast = []` and `[] ++ _ = _`
are both definitionally true.  The `cons` case applies the inductive
hypothesis and uses `List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil
(Walk.vertices_ne_nil p)` to unfold `(u :: p.vertices).dropLast`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_comp {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v w : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (q : Walk G v w),
      (p.comp q).vertices = p.vertices.dropLast ++ q.vertices
  -- `nil` arm: `[v].dropLast = []`, then `[] ++ q.vertices = q.vertices`.
  | _, _, _, .nil _ _, _ => rfl
  -- `cons` arm: IH + `List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil` to peel the head safely.
  | _, _, _, .cons _ _ _ p, q => by
      have hne : p.vertices ≠ [] := Walk.vertices_ne_nil p
      simp [Walk.comp, Walk.vertices, Walk.vertices_comp p q,
            List.dropLast_cons_of_ne_nil hne]

lean_blocks.code:
/-- Auxiliary: the source `u` of a walk `p : Walk G u v` is the head of
`p.vertices`, hence lies in `p.vertices`.  The `nil` case unfolds to
`u ∈ [u]`; the `cons` case unfolds to `u ∈ u :: rest`; both close by
`simp [Walk.vertices]`.  Used by `liftTo_hardInterventionOn`'s `cons`
recursion to extract `vMid ∉ W` from the cons-walk's `vertices.tail`-
avoidance hypothesis (the cons-walk's `.vertices.tail` definitionally
equals `p'.vertices`, whose head is `vMid`). -/
private lemma Walk.head_mem_vertices {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v), u ∈ p.vertices
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]

/-- Auxiliary: every walk's vertex list factors as `source :: tail`.
The `nil` case: `[u].tail = []` and `u :: [] = [u]`, so the equality is
definitional.  The `cons` case: `(u :: p'.vertices).tail = p'.vertices`,
so `u :: ((cons _ _ _ p').vertices.tail) = u :: p'.vertices = (cons _ _ _ p').vertices`,
again definitional.  Used by `vertices_directed_avoid_of_hardInterventionOn`'s
`cons` case to split `x ∈ p'.vertices` into "x equals the source vertex
`vMid`" / "x lies in `p'.vertices.tail`" via `List.mem_cons`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_eq_head_cons_tail {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v), p.vertices = u :: p.vertices.tail
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => rfl
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ _ => rfl

/-- **Subtask 3a:** every vertex of a *directed* walk in
`G.hardInterventionOn W hW`, except the source, avoids `W`.

The `.tail` carve-out is load-bearing: the source `u = u_0` is
unconstrained — it may or may not be in `W`.  Only the heads of the
edges (positions `1, 2, …, n` of `vertices`) must avoid `W`, because
`(u_i, u_{i+1}) ∈ E_{do(W)}` forces `u_{i+1} ∉ W` via the `e.2 ∉ W`
clause of `def_3_10` item iii's `Finset.filter`.

Proof: induction on `p`.  The `nil` case is vacuous
(`(.nil v _).vertices.tail = []`).  The `cons` case obtains
`a ∈ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).E` from the `IsDirectedWalk`
conjunct, extracts `vMid ∉ W` via `Finset.mem_filter.mp` and the
`ha_eq : a = (u, vMid)` head identification, then splits the membership
`x ∈ p'.vertices` into "x = vMid" (closed by the just-extracted
`vMid ∉ W`) and "x ∈ p'.vertices.tail" (closed by the IH applied to
`p'` and its directedness hypothesis). -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_directed_avoid_of_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      p.IsDirectedWalk → ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail, x ∉ W
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _, _, hx => by simp [Walk.vertices] at hx
  | _, _, .cons vMid a _ p', hp_dir, x, hx => by
      change x ∈ p'.vertices at hx
      obtain ⟨ha_eq, ha_E, hp'_dir⟩ := hp_dir
      have hvMid_notW : vMid ∉ W := by
        have hh := (Finset.mem_filter.mp ha_E).2
        rw [ha_eq] at hh
        exact hh
      rw [Walk.vertices_eq_head_cons_tail p'] at hx
      rcases List.mem_cons.mp hx with rfl | hx_tail
      · exact hvMid_notW
      · exact Walk.vertices_directed_avoid_of_hardInterventionOn p'
          hp'_dir x hx_tail

/-- **Subtask 3b:** rebuild a directed walk `p : Walk G u v` in the
intervened CDMG `G.hardInterventionOn W hW`, provided the source `u`
is itself a node of the intervention and every non-source vertex of
`p` avoids `W`.

Cell-by-cell: each `cons` step's `WalkStep` witness is built from the
`def_3_4`-item-ii data `a = (u, vMid) ∧ a ∈ G.E` (extracted from
`hp_dir`) together with `vMid ∉ W` (extracted from `hp_avoid` applied
to the head of the tail walk's vertex list, via
`Walk.head_mem_vertices`).  The recursive call's source-membership
hypothesis `hvMid_inHard` is re-derived in the `cons` case from
`G.hE_subset` applied to `a ∈ G.E` (giving `a.2 ∈ G.V`, hence
`vMid ∈ G.V`) and `vMid ∉ W` (placing `vMid` in the `G.V ∖ W`
right-disjunct of the intervention's `J ∪ V` carrier). -/
private def Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v),
      u ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW →
      p.IsDirectedWalk →
      (∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail, x ∉ W) →
      Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v
  | _, _, .nil v _, hu, _, _ => Walk.nil v hu
  | u, _, .cons vMid a _ p', _, hp_dir, hp_avoid =>
      have hvMid_notW : vMid ∉ W :=
        hp_avoid vMid (Walk.head_mem_vertices p')
      have hvMid_V : vMid ∈ G.V := by
        have hh := (G.hE_subset hp_dir.2.1).2
        rw [hp_dir.1] at hh
        exact hh
      have hvMid_inHard : vMid ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW := by
        change vMid ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)
        exact Finset.mem_union_right _
          (Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨hvMid_V, hvMid_notW⟩)
      have hStepNew : (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).WalkStep u a vMid := by
        refine Or.inl ⟨hp_dir.1, Or.inl ?_⟩
        refine Finset.mem_filter.mpr ⟨hp_dir.2.1, ?_⟩
        rw [hp_dir.1]
        exact hvMid_notW
      have hp'_avoid : ∀ x ∈ p'.vertices.tail, x ∉ W := fun y hy =>
        hp_avoid y (List.mem_of_mem_tail hy)
      Walk.cons vMid a hStepNew
        (Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn p' hvMid_inHard hp_dir.2.2 hp'_avoid)

/-- **Subtask 3c:** the `liftTo_hardInterventionOn` lift preserves
`IsDirectedWalk`.

The `nil` case is `trivial` (`(Walk.nil v _).IsDirectedWalk = True`).
The `cons` case reduces by the equation compiler to
`(Walk.cons vMid a hStepNew (p'.liftTo_hardInterventionOn …)).IsDirectedWalk`,
which by the `cons` clause of `IsDirectedWalk` decomposes as the
conjunction of (a) `a = (u, vMid)` from `hp_dir`, (b)
`a ∈ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).E` from `Finset.mem_filter.mpr`
packaging of `hp_dir.2.1` and the head-avoidance `vMid ∉ W`, and (c)
`(p'.liftTo…).IsDirectedWalk` from the IH applied to `p'` and the
re-derived recursive-call hypotheses. -/
private lemma Walk.isDirectedWalk_liftTo_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (hu : u ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW)
      (hp_dir : p.IsDirectedWalk)
      (hp_avoid : ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail, x ∉ W),
      (Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn (hW := hW) p hu hp_dir hp_avoid).IsDirectedWalk
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _, _, _ => trivial
  | _, _, .cons vMid a _ p', _, hp_dir, hp_avoid => by
      have hvMid_notW : vMid ∉ W :=
        hp_avoid vMid (Walk.head_mem_vertices p')
      have hvMid_V : vMid ∈ G.V := by
        have hh := (G.hE_subset hp_dir.2.1).2
        rw [hp_dir.1] at hh
        exact hh
      have hvMid_inHard : vMid ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW := by
        change vMid ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)
        exact Finset.mem_union_right _
          (Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨hvMid_V, hvMid_notW⟩)
      have hp'_avoid : ∀ x ∈ p'.vertices.tail, x ∉ W := fun y hy =>
        hp_avoid y (List.mem_of_mem_tail hy)
      refine ⟨hp_dir.1, ?_, ?_⟩
      · refine Finset.mem_filter.mpr ⟨hp_dir.2.1, ?_⟩
        rw [hp_dir.1]
        exact hvMid_notW
      · exact Walk.isDirectedWalk_liftTo_hardInterventionOn p'
          hvMid_inHard hp_dir.2.2 hp'_avoid
lean_blocks.explanation: This block is the `(⇐)` direction's *lift-into-the-intervention* toolkit. Two tiny prerequisites come first: `Walk.head_mem_vertices` (the source $u$ always sits at the head of `p.vertices`, hence is a member) and `Walk.vertices_eq_head_cons_tail` (the obvious factoring `p.vertices = u :: p.vertices.tail`). The three numbered subtasks are the real work. **3a**: a directed walk in `G_{do(W)}` automatically has every *non-source* vertex outside $W$ — this is because each edge $(u_i, u_{i+1}) \in E_{do(W)}$ comes from `Finset.filter` with predicate `e.2 ∉ W`, forcing every head $u_{i+1}$ out of $W$. The `.tail` carve-out is load-bearing: the source itself is unconstrained. **3b** is the inverse — `Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn` rebuilds a `Walk G u v` into a `Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v` provided the source lies in the intervention and every non-source vertex avoids $W$. The recursive case re-derives the head vertex's intervention membership from `G.hE_subset` (head in `G.V`) plus the avoidance hypothesis (head outside `W`), placing it in the `G.V \ W` summand of the carrier. **3c** certifies that the lift preserves `IsDirectedWalk`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
/-- Auxiliary: the source `u` of a walk `p : Walk G u v` is the head of
`p.vertices`, hence lies in `p.vertices`.  The `nil` case unfolds to
`u ∈ [u]`; the `cons` case unfolds to `u ∈ u :: rest`; both close by
`simp [Walk.vertices]`.  Used by `liftTo_hardInterventionOn`'s `cons`
recursion to extract `vMid ∉ W` from the cons-walk's `vertices.tail`-
avoidance hypothesis (the cons-walk's `.vertices.tail` definitionally
equals `p'.vertices`, whose head is `vMid`). -/
private lemma Walk.head_mem_vertices {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v), u ∈ p.vertices
  -- `nil`: `u ∈ [u]`.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]
  -- `cons`: `u ∈ u :: rest`.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ _ => by simp [Walk.vertices]

/-- Auxiliary: every walk's vertex list factors as `source :: tail`.
The `nil` case: `[u].tail = []` and `u :: [] = [u]`, so the equality is
definitional.  The `cons` case: `(u :: p'.vertices).tail = p'.vertices`,
so `u :: ((cons _ _ _ p').vertices.tail) = u :: p'.vertices = (cons _ _ _ p').vertices`,
again definitional.  Used by `vertices_directed_avoid_of_hardInterventionOn`'s
`cons` case to split `x ∈ p'.vertices` into "x equals the source vertex
`vMid`" / "x lies in `p'.vertices.tail`" via `List.mem_cons`. -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_eq_head_cons_tail {G : CDMG Node} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v), p.vertices = u :: p.vertices.tail
  -- `nil`: `[u] = u :: [].tail`-shaped, both sides equal `[u]`.
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => rfl
  -- `cons`: by definition `.vertices = u :: p'.vertices`.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ _ => rfl

/-- **Subtask 3a:** every vertex of a *directed* walk in
`G.hardInterventionOn W hW`, except the source, avoids `W`.

The `.tail` carve-out is load-bearing: the source `u = u_0` is
unconstrained — it may or may not be in `W`.  Only the heads of the
edges (positions `1, 2, …, n` of `vertices`) must avoid `W`, because
`(u_i, u_{i+1}) ∈ E_{do(W)}` forces `u_{i+1} ∉ W` via the `e.2 ∉ W`
clause of `def_3_10` item iii's `Finset.filter`.

Proof: induction on `p`.  The `nil` case is vacuous
(`(.nil v _).vertices.tail = []`).  The `cons` case obtains
`a ∈ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).E` from the `IsDirectedWalk`
conjunct, extracts `vMid ∉ W` via `Finset.mem_filter.mp` and the
`ha_eq : a = (u, vMid)` head identification, then splits the membership
`x ∈ p'.vertices` into "x = vMid" (closed by the just-extracted
`vMid ∉ W`) and "x ∈ p'.vertices.tail" (closed by the IH applied to
`p'` and its directedness hypothesis). -/
private lemma Walk.vertices_directed_avoid_of_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v),
      p.IsDirectedWalk → ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail, x ∉ W
  -- `nil` arm: `(.nil v _).vertices.tail = []`, so the universal is vacuous.
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _, _, hx => by simp [Walk.vertices] at hx
  -- `cons` arm: unpack `hp_dir`, then extract `vMid ∉ W` from the filtered edge.
  | _, _, .cons vMid a _ p', hp_dir, x, hx => by
      change x ∈ p'.vertices at hx
      obtain ⟨ha_eq, ha_E, hp'_dir⟩ := hp_dir
      -- `Finset.mem_filter.mp` exposes the `e.2 ∉ W` predicate; `ha_eq` identifies `e.2 = vMid`.
      have hvMid_notW : vMid ∉ W := by
        have hh := (Finset.mem_filter.mp ha_E).2
        rw [ha_eq] at hh
        exact hh
      -- Split `x ∈ p'.vertices` into "x = vMid (head)" vs "x ∈ tail" via `List.mem_cons`.
      rw [Walk.vertices_eq_head_cons_tail p'] at hx
      rcases List.mem_cons.mp hx with rfl | hx_tail
      · exact hvMid_notW
      -- IH on `p'` for the tail elements.
      · exact Walk.vertices_directed_avoid_of_hardInterventionOn p'
          hp'_dir x hx_tail

/-- **Subtask 3b:** rebuild a directed walk `p : Walk G u v` in the
intervened CDMG `G.hardInterventionOn W hW`, provided the source `u`
is itself a node of the intervention and every non-source vertex of
`p` avoids `W`.

Cell-by-cell: each `cons` step's `WalkStep` witness is built from the
`def_3_4`-item-ii data `a = (u, vMid) ∧ a ∈ G.E` (extracted from
`hp_dir`) together with `vMid ∉ W` (extracted from `hp_avoid` applied
to the head of the tail walk's vertex list, via
`Walk.head_mem_vertices`).  The recursive call's source-membership
hypothesis `hvMid_inHard` is re-derived in the `cons` case from
`G.hE_subset` applied to `a ∈ G.E` (giving `a.2 ∈ G.V`, hence
`vMid ∈ G.V`) and `vMid ∉ W` (placing `vMid` in the `G.V ∖ W`
right-disjunct of the intervention's `J ∪ V` carrier). -/
private def Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v),
      u ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW →
      p.IsDirectedWalk →
      (∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail, x ∉ W) →
      Walk (G.hardInterventionOn W hW) u v
  -- `nil` arm: reuse the source-membership in the intervention directly.
  | _, _, .nil v _, hu, _, _ => Walk.nil v hu
  -- `cons` arm: build the head step + recurse on the tail with the trimmed hypotheses.
  | u, _, .cons vMid a _ p', _, hp_dir, hp_avoid =>
      -- Head vertex avoids `W` because it appears at position 0 of `p'.vertices` (= tail of the cons walk).
      have hvMid_notW : vMid ∉ W :=
        hp_avoid vMid (Walk.head_mem_vertices p')
      -- `G.hE_subset` + `ha_eq` give `vMid ∈ G.V`.
      have hvMid_V : vMid ∈ G.V := by
        have hh := (G.hE_subset hp_dir.2.1).2
        rw [hp_dir.1] at hh
        exact hh
      -- Pack into the intervention's `G.V \ W` summand of the `J ∪ V` carrier.
      have hvMid_inHard : vMid ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW := by
        change vMid ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)
        exact Finset.mem_union_right _
          (Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨hvMid_V, hvMid_notW⟩)
      -- Build the new `WalkStep` witness for the head edge inside the intervention.
      have hStepNew : (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).WalkStep u a vMid := by
        refine Or.inl ⟨hp_dir.1, Or.inl ?_⟩
        refine Finset.mem_filter.mpr ⟨hp_dir.2.1, ?_⟩
        rw [hp_dir.1]
        exact hvMid_notW
      -- Restrict the avoidance hypothesis to `p'.vertices.tail` via `List.mem_of_mem_tail`.
      have hp'_avoid : ∀ x ∈ p'.vertices.tail, x ∉ W := fun y hy =>
        hp_avoid y (List.mem_of_mem_tail hy)
      Walk.cons vMid a hStepNew
        (Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn p' hvMid_inHard hp_dir.2.2 hp'_avoid)

/-- **Subtask 3c:** the `liftTo_hardInterventionOn` lift preserves
`IsDirectedWalk`.

The `nil` case is `trivial` (`(Walk.nil v _).IsDirectedWalk = True`).
The `cons` case reduces by the equation compiler to
`(Walk.cons vMid a hStepNew (p'.liftTo_hardInterventionOn …)).IsDirectedWalk`,
which by the `cons` clause of `IsDirectedWalk` decomposes as the
conjunction of (a) `a = (u, vMid)` from `hp_dir`, (b)
`a ∈ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).E` from `Finset.mem_filter.mpr`
packaging of `hp_dir.2.1` and the head-avoidance `vMid ∉ W`, and (c)
`(p'.liftTo…).IsDirectedWalk` from the IH applied to `p'` and the
re-derived recursive-call hypotheses. -/
private lemma Walk.isDirectedWalk_liftTo_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} {hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V} :
    ∀ {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (hu : u ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW)
      (hp_dir : p.IsDirectedWalk)
      (hp_avoid : ∀ x ∈ p.vertices.tail, x ∉ W),
      (Walk.liftTo_hardInterventionOn (hW := hW) p hu hp_dir hp_avoid).IsDirectedWalk
  -- `nil` arm: `(Walk.nil _ _).IsDirectedWalk = True`.
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _, _, _ => trivial
  -- `cons` arm: re-derive the same head data as in `liftTo_…`, then recurse via IH.
  | _, _, .cons vMid a _ p', _, hp_dir, hp_avoid => by
      have hvMid_notW : vMid ∉ W :=
        hp_avoid vMid (Walk.head_mem_vertices p')
      have hvMid_V : vMid ∈ G.V := by
        have hh := (G.hE_subset hp_dir.2.1).2
        rw [hp_dir.1] at hh
        exact hh
      have hvMid_inHard : vMid ∈ G.hardInterventionOn W hW := by
        change vMid ∈ (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)
        exact Finset.mem_union_right _
          (Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨hvMid_V, hvMid_notW⟩)
      have hp'_avoid : ∀ x ∈ p'.vertices.tail, x ∉ W := fun y hy =>
        hp_avoid y (List.mem_of_mem_tail hy)
      -- `IsDirectedWalk` on the cons cell unfolds to a triple matching the lift's structure.
      refine ⟨hp_dir.1, ?_, ?_⟩
      -- Re-prove the filtered `E`-membership for the head edge inside the intervention.
      · refine Finset.mem_filter.mpr ⟨hp_dir.2.1, ?_⟩
        rw [hp_dir.1]
        exact hvMid_notW
      · exact Walk.isDirectedWalk_liftTo_hardInterventionOn p'
          hvMid_inHard hp_dir.2.2 hp'_avoid
